prompt: Rank AAPL, MSFT, NVDA, AMZN, GOOGL, META, and TSLA by DCF upside % — (dcfPrice minus stockPrice) divided by stockPrice — as a horizontal bar chart from cheapest (most upside) to most expensive (most downside), with each ticker's EV/EBITDA multiple labeled on its bar so I can see which mega-caps look intrinsically undervalued versus which trade rich on enterprise multiples.
expectedReason: homepage showcase categorical charts need multi-row comparisons
responsePreview:
The horizontal bar chart above ranks the seven mega-caps from cheapest (most DCF upside) to most expensive (most DCF downside), with each bar labeled by its TTM EV/EBITDA. As of the June 28, 2026 DCF estimates, here is the exact ordering:

| Rank | Ticker | DCF Upside % | EV/EBITDA (TTM) |
|------|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 (Cheapest) | NVDA | +29.4% | 24.19x |
| 2 | MSFT | -16.4% | 13.91x |
| 3 | AAPL | -44.7% | 26.30x |
| 4 | META | -47.5% | 12.87x |
| 5 | AMZN | -58.8% | 13.86x |
| 6 | GOOGL | -60.3% | 18.89x |
| 7 (Most Expensive) | TSLA | -95.0% | 117.57x |

**Key takeaways**
- **NVDA** is the only name trading below its DCF estimate, showing ~29% upside despite a 24.2x EV/EBITDA.
- **TSLA** is the most expensive by a wide margin, with a ~95% DCF downside and an EV/EBITDA near 117.6x.
- **META** and **AMZN** carry the lowest EV/EBITDA multiples (~12.9x and ~13.9x) but still sit roughly 47–59% above their DCF values.
- **AAPL** looks rich on both metrics, with a ~45% DCF downside and a 26.3x EV/EBITDA.
- **MSFT** is the closest to fair value among the downside names, with a ~16% DCF downside and a 13.9x EV/EBITDA.
